title: Predecessors
tex_block_html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $<$ a total order of $J \cup V$. The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ are: \[\Pred^G_<(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC.\] We also put: \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_9 -- "Predecessors".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it compile both standalone (resolving its
% preamble via `main.tex`) and as part of `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_9
% title: Predecessors
\def\rowref{def\_3\_9}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_9}

\begin{Def}[Predecessors]
    \label{def-predecessors}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}. Let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$, read as a \emph{strict} total order in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order} (irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J \cup V$); the symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout. The element $v$ is left unconstrained by the LN -- in particular, the LN does \emph{not} impose $v \in J \cup V$ -- and we follow that literal stance below; note that the comparison $w < v$ is only directly given by $<$ when $v \in J \cup V$, since $<$ is supplied only as a total order of $J \cup V$. Adopting the convention of def \ref{not-cdmg}, item~1, that ``$w \in G$'' abbreviates ``$w \in J \cup V$'', we write the set-builder bodies below explicitly with $w \in J \cup V$.

    The set of \emph{predecessors} of $v$ in $G$ is
    \[
        \Pred^G_<(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC.
    \]
    We also put
    \[
        \Pred^G_\le(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.
    \]

    \emph{Notational quirk: the ``$\le$'' subscript vs.\ the strict body.} The subscript ``$\le$'' on $\Pred^G_\le$ marks the non-strict variant, but the set-builder body the LN writes is \emph{strict} (the membership condition is $w < v$, not $w \le v$), with the singleton $\{v\}$ adjoined separately. We implement this literal LN body $\Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$, \emph{not} the alternative $\lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w \le v \rC$; the two forms coincide whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while the reflexive-closure form $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$), and they diverge only in the corner case noted next.

    \emph{Corner case $v \notin J \cup V$.} Under the literal LN body, $w < v$ for $w \in J \cup V$ is not provided by the total order $<$ (whose domain is $J \cup V$), so the natural reading is $\Pred^G_<(v) = \emptyset$; the non-strict body's adjoined singleton then degenerately gives $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \{v\}$, so $v$ is its own non-strict predecessor even when $v$ lies outside the underlying node set of $G$. We follow the LN's lack of hypothesis on $v$ and do \emph{not} impose $v \in J \cup V$ here; downstream consumers that pattern-match on the shape of an element of $\Pred^G_<(v)$ or $\Pred^G_\le(v)$ may assume $v \in J \cup V$ at the point of use as a separate hypothesis.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg. Let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$, read as a strict total order in the sense of def def-topological-order (irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $J \cup V$); the symbol $<$ is read strictly throughout. The element $v$ is left unconstrained by the LN ‚Äì in particular, the LN does not impose $v \in J \cup V$ ‚Äì and we follow that literal stance below; note that the comparison $w < v$ is only directly given by $<$ when $v \in J \cup V$, since $<$ is supplied only as a total order of $J \cup V$. Adopting the convention of def not-cdmg, item 1, that ‚Äú$w \in G$‚Äù abbreviates ‚Äú$w \in J \cup V$‚Äù, we write the set-builder bodies below explicitly with $w \in J \cup V$.

The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ is \[ \Pred^G_<(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC. \] We also put \[ \Pred^G_\le(v) := \lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}. \]

Notational quirk: the ‚Äú$\le$‚Äù subscript vs.\ the strict body. The subscript ‚Äú$\le$‚Äù on $\Pred^G_\le$ marks the non-strict variant, but the set-builder body the LN writes is strict (the membership condition is $w < v$, not $w \le v$), with the singleton $\{v\}$ adjoined separately. We implement this literal LN body $\Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$, not the alternative $\lC w \in J \cup V \,|\, w \le v \rC$; the two forms coincide whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while the reflexive-closure form $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$), and they diverge only in the corner case noted next.

Corner case $v \notin J \cup V$. Under the literal LN body, $w < v$ for $w \in J \cup V$ is not provided by the total order $<$ (whose domain is $J \cup V$), so the natural reading is $\Pred^G_<(v) = \emptyset$; the non-strict body's adjoined singleton then degenerately gives $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \{v\}$, so $v$ is its own non-strict predecessor even when $v$ lies outside the underlying node set of $G$. We follow the LN's lack of hypothesis on $v$ and do not impose $v \in J \cup V$ here; downstream consumers that pattern-match on the shape of an element of $\Pred^G_<(v)$ or $\Pred^G_\le(v)$ may assume $v \in J \cup V$ at the point of use as a separate hypothesis.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `Pred` and `PredLE`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for this row in particular: the set-builder body `{w | w ‚àà G ‚àß lt w v}` invokes `w ‚àà G`, which via the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` reduces to `Finset` membership on `G.J ‚à™ G.V` ‚Äî and `Finset` operations require decidable equality on the element type. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not needed here and is left to downstream use sites that actually consume it.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `w ‚àà G` via Finset membership.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This block formalises the strict predecessor set $\Pred^G_<(v) = \{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$. Under `def_3_2`'s `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance, `w ‚àà G` unfolds to `w ‚àà G.J ‚à™ G.V`, so the set-builder body reads literally as the LN's $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\}$. The signature change versus the pre-refactor shape is the new `(_h : G.IsTotalOrder lt)` parameter sitting between `lt` and `v`: this is the `total_order_helper` refactor's central move, lifting the LN's *"let `<` be a total order of `J ‚à™ V`"* premise from prose comments into Lean's type contract so every caller must supply the witness. The leading underscore on `_h` is the standard Lean convention for an *explicitly bound but intentionally unused* binder ‚Äî it silences the `unusedVariables` linter while keeping the parameter positional (callers pass the witness as the third argument regardless of binder name; `PredLE` does this with its own `h`). The hypothesis is a phantom enforced at type-check, not consumed by the body.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: def_3_9 (post-refactor) ‚Äî strict predecessors with type-level total-order witness.
-- `Pred` is a top-level def under `namespace CDMG`, so callers write `G.Pred lt h v`.
def Pred (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node ‚Üí Node ‚Üí Prop)
    -- `_h` is the total-order hypothesis the refactor added ‚Äî explicit (so callers must
    -- supply it) but underscored to silence `unusedVariables` since the body never reads it.
    (_h : G.IsTotalOrder lt) (v : Node) : Set Node :=
  -- Literal LN body `{w ‚àà J ‚à™ V | w < v}`: `w ‚àà G` routes through def_3_2 to Finset membership.
  {w | w ‚àà G ‚àß lt w v}

lean_blocks.explanation: This block formalises the non-strict predecessor set $\Pred^G_\le(v) = \Pred^G_<(v) \cup \{v\}$ ‚Äî the strict predecessor set adjoined with the node itself. The body reads on the nose as the LN's literal spelling: rather than unfolding to `{w | w ‚àà G ‚àß (lt w v ‚à® w = v)}` or `{w | w ‚â§ v}`, it calls the named `G.Pred lt h v` and unions with the `Set Node` singleton `{v}` (`Set.instSingleton` auto-resolves because `‚à™`'s left operand is already a `Set Node`). The signature carries the same `(h : G.IsTotalOrder lt)` hypothesis as `Pred`, but here the binder is `h` *without* an underscore: the body threads it into `G.Pred lt h v`, filling `Pred`'s `_h` slot positionally. That's the convention's other half ‚Äî underscore means "phantom", no underscore means "forwarded or consumed". The corner case `v ‚àâ G` is preserved: `v ‚àà G.PredLE lt h v` *unconditionally*, even outside `J ‚à™ V`.

design_choices:
**An explicit `(h : G.IsTotalOrder lt)` hypothesis sits between `lt` and `v` on both `Pred` and `PredLE` ‚Äî the central move of the `total_order_helper` refactor.** Pre-refactor, `Pred G lt v` was well-typed for *any* binary relation `lt`, but the LN's $\Pred^G_<(v)$ is only well-defined when $<$ is a total order on $J \cup V$ (the LN block opens *"Let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$"*). The premise lived in 60+ lines of design-choice comments where the type checker could not enforce it ‚Äî exactly the failure pattern `verify_equivalence` item~1a is designed to catch ("hypothesis dropped from Lean's type contract; only documented in design comments") and the CONTENT example `verify_equivalence_strict` calls *"loosening a quantifier's domain"*. Adding `h` to the type contract closes the leak at the source: every downstream consumer is now forced to supply the total-order witness rather than smuggling the LN premise in at the use site. The pre-refactor `G.Pred lt v` and $\Pred^G_<(v)$ were not the same mathematical object even though they coincide on the LN's intended inputs; the post-refactor signature makes them coincide *by construction*.

**The hypothesis is `IsTotalOrder`, not the stronger `IsTopologicalOrder` ‚Äî even though every downstream consumer in chapters 4+ has the latter in scope.** The LN's premise *for this row* reads "let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$" ‚Äî nothing more. The parent-precedence conjunct of `IsTopologicalOrder` ($\forall v\,w,\,v \in \Pa^G(w) \Rightarrow lt\,v\,w$) is *not* required for the predecessor-set computation $\{w \in G \mid lt\,w\,v\}$ to be well-defined: any total order on $J \cup V$ suffices. Demanding the full `IsTopologicalOrder` here would over-constrain the few legitimate consumers of non-topological total orders ‚Äî chapter 5's ID-algorithm "preceding Markov blanket" slice carves $J \cup V$ via total-order content alone, and the factorisation reverse-ordering step manipulates a total order qua total order. Consumers that *do* hold a `G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` witness reach `IsTotalOrder` via the first projection `(h_topo : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt).1` ‚Äî one step, no plumbing. This matches the helper-vs-full-predicate layering set up in `def_3_8`'s `IsTotalOrder` helper, and keeps `Pred` LN-faithful to *this row*'s literal phrasing rather than to its typical downstream call context.

**The `_h` vs `h` underscore convention threads the hypothesis through positionally without consuming it in `Pred`'s body.** `Pred`'s body ‚Äî `{w | w ‚àà G ‚àß lt w v}` ‚Äî never inspects the total-order witness; it is a pure type-level contract whose role is to *force the caller* to supply a proof, not to be consumed by the predecessor-set computation. The parameter is bound with the leading-underscore name `_h`, Lean's / Mathlib's standard convention for "explicitly bound but intentionally unused": this silences the `unusedVariables` linter while keeping the parameter positional, so callers pass the witness as the third positional argument regardless of binder name. `PredLE`'s body, by contrast, *does* reference its hypothesis ‚Äî it threads `h` into the call `G.Pred lt h v`, filling `Pred`'s `_h` slot ‚Äî so there the binder is named `h` without the underscore. That asymmetry is informative: a reader scanning the two signatures can tell at a glance which body forwards the witness and which holds it inertly. The hypothesis is plumbing, not data; the underscore makes that visible.

**`lt : Node ‚Üí Node ‚Üí Prop` remains an explicit external argument, not a `[LT Node]` typeclass or a bundled structure field.** The LN's subscript $<$ in $\Pred^G_<(v)$ is a *parameter* the predecessor set is computed against, not a structural feature of nodes. A `[LT Node]` instance would glue a single canonical strict order to the *type* `Node`, but a single `Node` type underlies every CDMG and each graph admits many topological orders. A bundled structure (`structure TopologicalOrder G where lt + props`) would conflate the parameter convention with `claim_3_2`'s existence claim and force every consumer who just wants to *use* a chosen `lt` to package it first. The post-refactor signature keeps `lt` raw and adds the property as a separate `Prop`-valued hypothesis ‚Äî same shape as `def_3_8`'s `IsTopologicalOrder`.

**Two top-level defs ‚Äî strict `Pred` and non-strict `PredLE` ‚Äî rather than a single operator parameterised on `<`/`‚â§` or `PredLE` as a thin notation wrapper.** The LN explicitly introduces *both* $\Pred^G_<$ and $\Pred^G_\le$, and both names recur under that distinction in later chapters: CBN factorisation and do-calculus reach for the strict form, while iSCM recursion ("values determined up to and including $v$") reaches for the non-strict one. Folding the choice into `def Pred (G) (lt) (h) (strict : Bool) (v)` would force every call site to thread a `Bool` and peel off a `match` before reasoning about set membership; a pure `notation` abbreviation would leave `PredLE` un-`@[simp]`-able as its own head symbol. Both downsides are avoided by exposing two named `def`s ‚Äî the second is a one-line wrapper `G.Pred lt h v ‚à™ {v}`, so the duplication cost is negligible.

**Output type `Set Node`, not `Finset Node` or the subtype `‚Ü•(G.J ‚à™ G.V)`.** The chapter's family-set primitives (`Pa`, `Ch`, `Sib`, `Anc`, `Desc`, `Sc`, `Dist` from `def_3_5`) all live in `Set Node`, and downstream uses freely intersect predecessor sets with them (e.g. $\Pred^G_<(v) \cap \Pa^G(v)$ in do-calculus, $\Pred^G_<(v) \cap V$ in CBN factorisation). A `Finset Node` carrier would thread a `Decidable (lt w v)` instance through every call site ‚Äî the LN never picks a decidable representative for $<$, and the new total-order hypothesis `h` is a bare `Prop` that does not produce one ‚Äî and would force `Finset.coe` round-trips against the ambient family sets. The subtype carrier would force `Subtype.val` coercions throughout and make `PredLE`'s adjoined `{v}` ill-typed in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$. The LN-promised finiteness is still recoverable downstream ‚Äî `G.J ‚à™ G.V` *is* a `Finset` ‚Äî but is not exposed at the API surface here.

**`PredLE`'s body is the literal LN spelling `G.Pred lt h v ‚à™ {v}`, not a reflexive-closure form `{w | w ‚àà G ‚àß (lt w v ‚à® w = v)}` or `{w | w ‚â§ v}`.** The LN subscript reads $\le$ but the body it actually writes is *strict* ‚Äî $\{w \in J \cup V \mid w < v\} \cup \{v\}$, with the singleton adjoined unconditionally. The two forms agree whenever $v \in J \cup V$ (irreflexivity of $<$ keeps $v$ out of the strict body, while $w \le v$ would pick it up via $v \le v$) and diverge only in the corner case $v \notin J \cup V$: under the literal LN body the adjoined `{v}` carries no `v ‚àà G` guard, so `v ‚àà G.PredLE lt h v` *unconditionally*. The new hypothesis `h` does not change this: `h` constrains `lt` on $J \cup V$, not the surrounding ambient `Node` type, so the set-theoretic semantics on nodes outside `G` is identical to the pre-refactor reading. The body is spelled with the named `G.Pred` rather than unfolded ‚Äî it reads on the nose as "the strict predecessor set plus $v$", and downstream proofs holding `hw : w ‚àà G.Pred lt h v` can apply `Or.inl hw` to obtain `w ‚àà G.PredLE lt h v` with no intermediate `Set.mem_setOf` unfolding. The singleton `{v}` resolves to `Set.instSingleton` since the left operand of `‚à™` is `Set Node`; `‚à™ {v}` is preferred over `insert v (G.Pred lt h v)` because it reads most directly as "predecessors plus the node itself", and `Set.mem_union` / `Set.mem_singleton_iff` are the natural destructors. Naming-wise, `Pred` and `PredLE` are namespaced under `CDMG` (enabling `G.Pred lt h v`), use Mathlib `PascalCase`, and the `LE` suffix signals the non-strict variant.
